+ "Evaluates the condition operand and executes exactly one of its two regions at runtime. If the condition is true, the then region executes; otherwise the else region executes. Each region must terminate with a yield op whose operand types match the op's result types in count and element type. The yielded values become the results of the if op.\n\nThe else region is optional when the op produces no results. If results are declared, an else region must be present to provide values on the false path.\n\nExample:\n\nIn this program, if `%cond` is true, `%r` takes the value produced by `add_v1`; otherwise it takes the value produced by `sub_v1`."
+ "Extracts slices from `input` at the multi-dimensional positions specified by `indices`.  Each length-Q vector in `indices` selects a position in the first Q dimensions of `input`; Q is `indices.shape[-1]` and must satisfy `Q <= rank(input)`.\n\n`output` has the same element type as `input` and its shape is:\n\n- When `Q == rank(input)`: each index vector selects a single scalar; `output.shape == indices.shape[:-1]`.\n\n- When `Q < rank(input)`: each index vector selects a `(rank(input)-Q)`- dimensional slice; `output.shape == indices.shape[:-1] + input.shape[Q:]`.\n\n**Supported element types**\n\n- `input` / `output`: any floating-point, integer, complex, or boolean type. - `index`: signed integer.\n\n**Constraints**\n\n- `indices.shape[-1]` must be static. - `indices.shape[-1] <= rank(input)`. - `output.shape == indices.shape[:-1] + input.shape[indices.shape[-1]:]`.\n\n**Index semantics**\n\nEach raw index value must fall in `[-s_i, s_i)` where `s_i = input.shape[i]`.  Negative values are wrapped once by adding `s_i`.  Indices outside this range are out-of-contract; well-formed programs must keep indices in range.\n\n**Examples**\n\nGather individual elements (full-rank, Q == N == 2):\n\nGather rows (partial-rank, Q=1, N=2):"
+ "Gathers values from `$input` along a single `$axis` at positions given by `$indices`, producing `$output` with the same shape as `$indices` and the same element type as `$input`.\n\n`$input`, `$indices`, and `$output` must all have the same rank R. `$axis` is a 0-D scalar index in the range `[-R, R)`.\n\nThe semantics are:\n\nFor a 3-D input:\n\nOn all axes other than `$axis`, `$input` and `$indices` must have the same size.\n\n**Output type:** `output.shape == indices.shape`, `output.dtype == input.dtype`.\n\nExamples:\n- input (3, 4, 5) float32, indices (3, 2, 5) si32, axis=1\n→ output (3, 2, 5) float32\n\n- input (6,) i8,           indices (6,) si16,      axis=0 → output (6,) i8\n\n- input (2, 3, 4) bool,    indices (2, 3, 4) si32,  axis=2 → output (2, 3, 4) bool"

+ "Layout: NCDHW. `input` has shape `(N, C, D, H, W)`. `kernel_size`, `stride`, and `dilation` are rank-1 tensors with exactly three elements corresponding to the spatial dimensions (D, H, W). `ceil_mode` is a scalar boolean tensor.\n\n**Output shape.** Let `dilated_K_i = dilation[i] * (kernel_size[i] - 1) + 1`. For each spatial dimension `i` with input size `L_in`:\n\nThe output has shape `(N, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)`.\n\n**Per-element computation.** For output element `(n, c, d, h, w)`:\n\n**NaN and ±inf propagation.** Pooling selects a value from the input without arithmetic. For floating-point element types: if any value in the kernel window is NaN, the result is NaN; if the window contains ±∞ but no NaN, the result is the maximum value according to IEEE 754 total order."

+ "Materializes a compile-time constant attribute into an SSA value that can flow through the graph and be used at runtime. The result is immutable and may be folded or deduplicated by the compiler.\n\nTwo attribute forms are accepted:\n\n- `DenseElementsAttr`: constant data is stored inline in the IR. The element values are encoded directly and may use a splat (single value broadcast to all elements) or a per-element encoding.\n\n- `DenseResourceElementsAttr`: constant data is stored externally and referenced by a named blob handle. The blob is carried alongside the IR in a `dialect_resources` section and is loaded at runtime.\n\nIn both cases the result type is exactly the type of the attribute, so `InferTypeOpInterface` is used to elide the redundant type annotation from the printed form.\n\nExamples:"
+ "Performs 2D max pooling over a 4D input tensor in NCHW layout (`$input` has shape `[N, C, H_in, W_in]`). Each channel is processed independently. No padding is applied.\n\n**Output shape**\n\nThe output has shape `[N, C, H_out, W_out]` where the spatial dimensions are computed as follows. Let `d_h = dilation[0]`, `d_w = dilation[1]`, `k_h = kernel_size[0]`, `k_w = kernel_size[1]`, `s_h = stride[0]`, `s_w = stride[1]`. The effective (dilated) kernel size along each spatial dimension is:\n\nWhen `$ceil_mode` is `false` (floor mode):\n\nWhen `$ceil_mode` is `true` (ceil mode):\n\n**Pooling computation**\n\nFor each output element at position `(n, c, h, w)`, the result is the maximum over the receptive field:\n\nAll input positions in the receptive field must lie within `[0, H_in)` and `[0, W_in)`. The dilation factor `d` inserts `d-1` gaps between kernel elements; `d=1` is equivalent to no dilation.\n\n**Special values**\n\n- NaN in the input propagates to the output: if any input element in the receptive field is NaN, the output is NaN.\n\n- `+inf` and `-inf` propagate normally through the max operation. - Output dtype always matches the input dtype (`$output` has the same element type as `$input`).\n\n**Constraints**\n\n- `$input` and `$output` are 4D tensors with the same element type. - `$kernel_size`, `$stride`, and `$dilation` are 1D tensors with exactly 2 elements of integer type, corresponding to the H and W spatial dimensions.\n\n- `$stride` values must each be > 0. - `$dilation` values must each be > 0. - `$ceil_mode` is a scalar boolean tensor. - Supported element types for `$input`/`$output`: floating-point and, and integer types."
+ "Performs a 3D convolution of `input` with `weight`.\n\nTensor layouts:\n- `input`:  (N, C_in, D, H, W)\n- `weight`: (C_out, C_in/groups, k_D, k_H, k_W)\n- `output`: (N, C_out, D_out, H_out, W_out)\n\nOutput spatial size:\nD_out = floor((D - (dilation_D * (k_D - 1)) - 1) / stride_D + 1)\nH_out = floor((H - (dilation_H * (k_H - 1)) - 1) / stride_H + 1)\nW_out = floor((W - (dilation_W * (k_W - 1)) - 1) / stride_W + 1)\n\nPadding is not part of this op. Apply a padding op to the input before passing it here.\n\n`stride` and `dilation` are rank-1 tensors of length 3 with (D, H, W) ordering. `groups` is a 0-D tensor. C_in must be divisible by groups, and C_in/groups must equal `weight`'s second dimension."
+ "Performs windowed summation over a 4D input tensor with layout [N, C, H, W].\n\nFor each output position (i, j):\nout[n, c, i, j] = sum_{p=0}^{k_h-1} sum_{q=0}^{k_w-1}\ninput[n, c, i*s_h + p*d_h, j*s_w + q*d_w]\n\nwhere s_h, s_w are the height and width strides, d_h, d_w are the height and width dilation factors, and k_h, k_w are the kernel height and width. The output spatial dimensions are:\nH_out = floor((H - d_h*(k_h-1) - 1) / s_h) + 1\nW_out = floor((W - d_w*(k_w-1) - 1) / s_w) + 1\n\nFor floating-point inputs, NaN in any contributing window element propagates to the output. Positive or negative infinity in any contributing element propagates to the output.\n\nFor integer inputs, arithmetic uses the element type's natural width and wraps on overflow.\n\nFor complex inputs, the summation applies independently to the real and imaginary parts.\n\nThe output element type always matches the input element type."
+ "Quantizes a float tensor to an MX float format, computing the quantization scale dynamically from the input data. Returns both the quantized tensor and the computed scale.\n\nRanked tensors are verified statically (shape, block_sizes length). Unranked tensors (e.g. from placeholders with unknown shape) are accepted; rank-dependent checks are deferred to runtime.\n\n**Supported formats (last-dimension blocking only):** - MX: `f4e2m1`, `f6e2m3`, `f6e3m2`, `f8e4m3`, or `f8e5m2` output with `f8e8m0` scale; `block_sizes[rank-1]` must be 32.\n\n`block_sizes` is a per-dimension array of length equal to the input rank; all entries must be 1 except the last.\n\nShape relationships:\n\nExample:"
+ "Replace existing unique IDs with the same tag instead of appending"
+ "Represents a pre-compiled computation that has been offloaded to the compute delegate identified by `$delegate_id`.\n\nDuring compilation, the delegate compiler replaces the body of each segmented `IsolatedGroupOp` with one or more delegate ops. The `$resource` attribute is an opaque binary blob produced by the delegate's ahead-of-time compiler — its format is implementation-defined and interpreted only by the corresponding delegate runtime.\n\nAt runtime, the CoreAI executor dispatches each delegate op to the backend registered under `$delegate_id`, passing `$resource` and the live `$inputs` tensors. The delegate runtime initializes any backend-specific state, executes the compiled payload, and writes the results into `$outputs`.\n\n`$arg_attrs` and `$res_attrs` carry per-operand and per-result metadata (e.g., `udml.intent` annotations) that control how the runtime allocates I/O buffers. Their array lengths must match the operand and result counts respectively.\n\n`$delegate_attrs` is an optional dictionary for delegate-specific key-value configuration that does not fit into the per-operand or per-result attribute arrays.\n\n`$lifecycle` indicates the lifecycle phase of the delegate op, which controls initialization and teardown behavior in the runtime."
+ "Represents a pre-compiled computation that has been offloaded to the compute delegate identified by `$delegate_id`.\n\nDuring compilation, the delegate compiler replaces the body of each segmented `IsolatedGroupOp` with one or more delegate ops. The `$resource` attribute is an opaque binary blob produced by the delegate's ahead-of-time compiler — its format is implementation-defined and interpreted only by the corresponding delegate runtime.\n\nAt runtime, the CoreAI executor dispatches each delegate op to the backend registered under `$delegate_id`, passing `$resource` and the live `$inputs` tensors. The delegate runtime initializes any backend-specific state, executes the compiled payload, and writes the results into `$outputs`.\n\n`$arg_attrs` and `$res_attrs` carry per-operand and per-result metadata (e.g., `udml.intent` annotations) that control how the runtime allocates I/O buffers. Their array lengths must match the operand and result counts respectively.\n\n`$lifecycle` indicates the lifecycle phase of the delegate op, which controls initialization and teardown behavior in the runtime."
+ "Scatters values from `updates` into `input` along `axis` at positions specified by `indices`, producing `output`. The operation is the inverse of `gather_along_axis_v1`.\n\n**Operand constraints:** - `input`: ranked tensor of rank R ≥ 1 with a floating-point, complex, integer, or boolean element type. - `indices`: ranked tensor of rank R with signed-integer element type. For every dimension d ≠ axis: `indices.shape[d] == input.shape[d]`. Each value in `indices` must be in the range `[-input.shape[axis], input.shape[axis])`.\n\n- `updates`: ranked tensor with the same shape and rank as `indices`, and the same element type as `input`.\n\n- `axis`: 0-D tensor of signed-integer. Negative values are supported: effective axis = `axis + rank(input)` when `axis < 0`.\n\n- `output`: same shape and element type as `input`.\n\n**Output computation:**\n\nLet `D = rank(input) - 1` and let `axis` denote the (non-negative) effective scatter axis. For each multi-index `(p_0, ..., p_{axis-1}, i, p_{axis+1}, ..., p_D)` into `indices`, let:\n\nThe output at position `idx` along `axis` is updated as follows, depending on `scatter_mode`:\n| Mode   | Update rule |\n|--------|-------------|\n| update | `output[..., idx, ...] = updates[..., i, ...]` |\n| add    | `output[..., idx, ...] += updates[..., i, ...]` |\n| sub    | `output[..., idx, ...] -= updates[..., i, ...]` |\n| mul    | `output[..., idx, ...] *= updates[..., i, ...]` |\n| div    | `output[..., idx, ...] /= updates[..., i, ...]` |\n| max    | `output[..., idx, ...] = max(output[..., idx, ...], updates[..., i, ...])` |\n| min    | `output[..., idx, ...] = min(output[..., idx, ...], updates[..., i, ...])` |\n\nAll positions in `output` not targeted by any `(idx, ...)` pair retain the corresponding value from `input`. The `output` is therefore initialized as a copy of `input`.\n\n**3-D example (all axes, update mode):**\n\n**Duplicate indices:** When multiple elements of `indices` resolve to the same output position, the behaviour depends on `scatter_mode`. For `update` mode the result at that position is implementation-defined (any one of the conflicting `updates` values may be written). For all reduction modes (`add`, `sub`, `mul`, `div`, `max`, `min`) every contributing `updates` value is applied, but the order of application is implementation-defined.\n\n**Supported element types for `input`, `updates`, and `output`:** Floating-point types, complex types, integer types, and boolean."
+ "Selects elements from `$a` or `$b` based on the corresponding element in `$condition`:\n\nWhere `condition[i]` is `true`, the result takes the value from `$a`; where `false`, from `$b`. The selection is purely elementwise — each position is independent.\n\nFor floating-point types, values are copied exactly with no rounding or conversion. Special values (NaN, ±inf, ±0.0) are preserved as-is from whichever branch is selected — the non-selected branch's value is discarded without inspection.\n\nFor integer and boolean types, values are likewise copied without modification.\n\nFor complex types, both real and imaginary parts are taken from the selected branch as a unit — there is no per-component selection."
+ "Selects elements from `$a` or `$b` based on the corresponding element in `$condition`:\n\nWhere `condition[i]` is `true`, the result takes the value from `$a`; where `false`, from `$b`. The selection is purely elementwise — each position is independent.\n\nFor floating-point types, values are copied exactly with no rounding or conversion. Special values (NaN, ±inf, ±0.0) are preserved as-is from whichever branch is selected — the non-selected branch's value is discarded without inspection.\n\nFor integer and boolean types, values are likewise copied without modification.\n\nFor complex types, both real and imaginary parts are taken from the selected branch as a unit — there is no per-component selection.\n\nThis is the broadcasting variant of `coreai.where`. The inputs `$condition`, `$a`, and `$b` must have broadcastable shapes; the result shape is the broadcast of all three."
+ "Splits `$input` along dimension `$dim` into a variadic number of output tensors, where each output shares the same rank, element type, and non-split dimensions as `$input`.\n\nThe behavior depends on the rank of `$split_size_or_sections`:\n\n**Split-by-size** (`$split_size_or_sections` is 0D):\nThe scalar value `s` divides `$input` into `ceil(input.shape[dim] / s)`\nchunks along `$dim`. Each chunk has size `s` except the last, which\ncontains the remainder `input.shape[dim] % s` if it is nonzero.\n`s` must be >= 1.\n\n**Split-by-sections** (`$split_size_or_sections` is 1D):\nEach element specifies the size of the corresponding output tensor\nalong `$dim`. All section sizes must be non-negative, and their sum\nmust equal `input.shape[dim]`.\n\n`$dim` supports negative indexing: a negative value `d` is interpreted as `d + rank(input)`.\n\nA split producing a single output whose result type matches the input type folds to an identity (input passes through unchanged).\n\nExamples:\n- input (6, 4), size=2, dim=0\n→ 3 outputs: (2, 4), (2, 4), (2, 4)\n\n- input (10,), size=3, dim=0 → 4 outputs: (3,), (3,), (3,), (1,)\n\n- input (3, 8), sections=[2, 3, 3], dim=1 → 3 outputs: (3, 2), (3, 3), (3, 3)\n\n- input (2, 3, 12), size=3, dim=2 → 4 outputs: (2, 3, 3), (2, 3, 3), (2, 3, 3), (2, 3, 3)"

+ "Writes slices from `updates` into `input` at the multi-dimensional positions specified by `indices`.  This is the dual of `aicode.primitive.gather_nd_v1`: where gather reads, scatter writes.\n\nLet Q = `indices.shape[-1]` (must be static, `Q <= rank(input)`). For every multi-index `i` spanning `indices.shape[:-1]`, the update is:\n\nAll untargeted positions in `output` retain the corresponding value from `input`.  `output` has the same shape and element type as `input`.\n\n**Update function region**\n\nThe `update_fn` region receives two rank-0 tensor arguments — the existing value and the incoming update — and yields the value to write. Yielding the update argument gives replace semantics; if two index vectors target the same position the result is undefined.\n\n**Supported element types**\n\n- `input` / `updates` / `output`: any floating-point, integer, complex, or boolean type.\n\n- `indices`: signed integer.\n\n**Constraints**\n\n- `indices.shape[-1]` must be static. - `indices.shape[-1] <= rank(input)`. - `updates.shape == indices.shape[:-1] + input.shape[indices.shape[-1]:]`.\n\n**Index semantics**\n\nEach raw index value must fall in `[-s_i, s_i)` where `s_i = input.shape[i]`.  Negative values are wrapped once by adding `s_i`.  Indices outside this range are out-of-contract; well-formed programs must keep indices in range.\n\n**Examples**\n\nScatter scalar elements into a rank-1 tensor (replace mode):\n\nScatter rows into a rank-2 tensor (partial-rank, Q=1, N=2):"
+ "Writes the values of `$update` into the slice region of `$input` defined by per-dimension [start, end) half-open intervals and a non-zero step, producing `$output`. This is the setter counterpart of `coreai.slice` / `slice_v1`.\n\nOperands:\n\n- `$input`: Ranked source tensor with rank R. Its values are copied to `$output` before the update region is written.\n\n- `$start_indices`: 1-D index tensor of exactly R elements. `$start_indices[i]` is the first index (inclusive) to write along dimension `i`.\n\n- `$end_indices`: 1-D index tensor of exactly R elements. `$end_indices[i]` is the exclusive bound of the write range along dimension `i`.\n\n- `$strides`: 1-D index tensor of exactly R non-zero elements. `$strides[i]` is the step between consecutive written elements along dimension `i`. Must not contain zeros.\n\n- `$update`: Tensor whose values are written into the slice region of `$input`.\n\nIndex resolution (per dimension `i`, let `N = $input.shape[i]`):\n\n1. Wrap negative indices once: if a start or end value is strictly less than the lower bound of its valid range, add `N` to it exactly once.\n\n2. Clamp to valid range: - For a positive stride (`s > 0`): start is clamped to `[0, N]`; end is clamped to `[0, N]`.\n\n- For a negative stride (`s < 0`): start is clamped to `[0, N-1]`; end is clamped to `[-1, N-1]`.\n\n3. Compute slice size: the number of elements in the slice along dimension `i` is `max(0, ceil((resolved_end - resolved_start) / s))`. The shape of `$update` must equal these computed slice sizes.\n\nResults:\n\n- `$output`: Tensor with the same element type, rank, and shape as `$input`. Elements outside the slice region are copied unchanged from `$input`; elements within the slice region are replaced by the corresponding values from `$update`.\n\nConstraints:\n\n- `$start_indices`, `$end_indices`, and `$strides` must all have the same element type and each have length equal to the rank of `$input`.\n\n- No stride may be zero. - The shape of `$update` must match the resolved slice shape. - The output element type always matches the input element type.\n\nExamples:\n\n1D update — `destination[2:8:1] = source`:\n\n2D patch update — `destination[1:4, 2:5] = source`:"

+ "`aicode.primitive.expand_dims_v1` inserts one or more new unit-length dimensions into a tensor or scalar. The output has the same element type and values as `$input`, with rank equal to `rank($input) + len($axes)`.\n\n**Output Shape:** The output rank is `rank($input) + len($axes)`. Each value in `$axes` specifies a position in the *output* shape at which to insert a new dimension of size 1. Negative axes are first normalized to positive indices by adding the output rank. The axes are then sorted and processed in ascending order, with each insertion shifting subsequent dimension indices up by one:\n\n**Operands:** - `$input`: An AICode tensor of any supported element type (floating-point, integer, bool, or complex). Scalars (rank-0 tensors) are accepted.\n\n- `$axes`: A 1-D AICode index tensor. Each element specifies a position in the *output* tensor at which to insert a new unit-length dimension. Negative values index from the end of the output rank. After normalization, all axis values must be unique and within `[0, rank($input) + len($axes) - 1]`. `$axes` must be non-empty.\n\n**Results:** - `$output`: An AICode tensor with the same element type as `$input` and rank equal to `rank($input) + len($axes)`. Dimensions at positions listed in `$axes` have size 1; the remaining dimensions are those of `$input` in order.\n\n**Examples:**"

- "Control Flow op for branching on a condition"
- "FlatBuffers 1.12.0"
- "Gather tensor slices from `input` at locations specified in `indices`"
- "Incompatible element type for ANE: expected fp16, si8, or ui8"
- "Incompatible element type for ANE: expected fp16, si8, ui8, si16, or ui16"
- "Incompatible element type for ANE: expected fp16, si8, ui8, si16, ui16, si32, or ui32"
- "Incompatible element type for ANE: expected fp16, si8, ui8, si32, or ui32"
- "ODIE-1.0"
- "Performs 3D convolution operation on input tensor with weight tensor.\n\nTensor formats:\n- Input:  (N, C_in, D, H, W)\nwhere N = batch size, C_in = input channels, D = depth, H = height, W = width\n\n- Weight: (C_out, C_in_per_group, k_d, k_h, k_w) where C_out = output channels, C_in_per_group = C_in / groups, k_d = kernel depth, k_h = kernel height, k_w = kernel width\n\n- Output: (N, C_out, D_out, H_out, W_out) where D_out, H_out, and W_out are computed based on stride, dilation, and input spatial dimensions\n\nFor grouped convolution (groups > 1):\n- Input channels are divided into groups\n- C_in_per_group = C_in / groups\n- Each group performs convolution independently\n\nNote: bias is not part of this operation."
- "Performs windowed summation over a 4D input tensor [N, C, H, W].\n\nFor each output position (i, j):\nout[n, c, i, j] = Σ_{p=0}^{k_h-1} Σ_{q=0}^{k_w-1}\ninput[n, c, i*s_h + p*d_h, j*s_w + q*d_w]\n\nOutput shape: H_out = ⌊(H - d_h*(k_h-1) - 1) / s_h⌋ + 1 W_out = ⌊(W - d_w*(k_w-1) - 1) / s_w⌋ + 1\n\nkernel_size, strides, and dilation must have exactly 2 elements and be of unsigned integer type (ui16 or ui32)."
- "Pooling is used for spatial downsampling to reduce the dimensionality of input feature map. The downsampling is performed by selecting the maximum value among the range of values in the selected kernel size.\n\nFor an input sized `(N, C, D, H, W)` and kernel sized `(Kd, Kh, Kw)`, the output size `(N, C, Dout, Hout, Wout)` is calculated as:\n\nIf dilation is non-zero, the input window under a kernel gets dilated before applying the kernel. If ceil mode is True, ceil operation is used (instead of floor) to compute the output shape.\n\nkernel_size, stride, and dilation will have exactly three elements corresponding to the three spatial dimensions (D, H, W)."
- "Pooling is used for spatial downsampling to reduce the dimensionality of input feature map. The downsampling is performed by selecting the maximum value among the range of values in the selected kernel size.\n\nFor an input sized `(N, C, H, W)` and kernel sized `(Kh, Kw)`, the output size `(N, C, Hout, Wout)` is calculated as:\n\nIf dilation is non-zero, the input window under a kernel gets dilated before applying the kernel. If ceil mode is True, ceil operation is used (instead of floor) to compute the output shape.\n\nkernel_size, stride, and dilation will have exactly two elements corresponding to the two spatial dimensions (H, W)."
- "Return a tensor of elements selected from either input or other, depending on condition. Basically the operations does this\n\nwhere `condition`, `a` and `b` have broadcastable shapes."
- "Return a tensor of elements selected from either input or other, depending on condition. Basically the operations does this results[i] = condition[i] ? a[i]: b[i];"
- "Scatter updates into input tensor at locations specified by indices with custom update function."
- "Splits a given tensor (`$input`) along a given dimension (`$dim`) according to given split sizes/sections (`$split_size_or_sections`), producing a variadic set of tensors (`$result`).;"
- "This op will extract values from `$input` along `$axis` to produce `$output`. The locations to gather are informed by `$indices`.\n\nThe rank of `$indices` needs to be the same as the rank of `$input`. The output is produced by:\n\nwhere\n\nUse a 3-D tensor as an example:\n\n`$output` has the same type as `$input` and its shape is the same as `$indices`."
- "This op will scatter values from `updates` into `input` along `axis` and produce `output`. The locations to scatter are informed by `indices`. How the new value is produced is specified by `mode`.\n\nThe rank of `indices` needs to be the same as the rank of `input` and `updates`. The output is produced by\n\nwhere\n\nThe above rule assume `mode` is \"update\", and if the mode is \"add\" the update rule will be `+=` instead of `=`.\n\nUse a 3-D tensor as an example:\n\n`output` has the same type and shape as `input`."
- "This operation represents a group of computations that has been delegated to the compute delegate specified by `delegate_id` attribute. The delegate has produced a $resource blob which is an array of bytes."
- "This operation represents a group of computations that has been delegated to the compute delegate specified by `delegate_id` attribute. The delegate has produced a $resource blob which is an array of bytes.\n\nDelegates may specify additional attribute(s) in the $delegate_attrs opaque attribute which will be propagated through to the runtime."
- "Update the slice of the destination tensor with the source tensor."
- "_op_id"
- "`expand_dims` accepts a scalar or tensor `input` and a 1-D list of `axes`. For each axis in `axes`, this operation will insert a new dimension of unit length. The op's `output` will have the same data as `input`, but with rank = rank(`input`) + len(`axes`).\n\nNegative values in `axes` will index from the end of rank of `input`."
- "fused_loc_metadata"
- "h19"
- "operation must have IsolatedFromAbove trait for debug serialization"
